claimText: The Wildlife Protection Act (WPA) 2022 Amendment reduced the number of schedules to four, designating Schedule III for protected plants and Schedule IV for CITES species.
evidenceExcerpt: WPA 2022 Amendment reduced schedules to 4: Sch I (highest protection), Sch II (lesser protection), Sch III (plants), Sch IV (CITES species).
examUse: Statement-based question on the features of the WPA 2022 Amendment.
distractorHooks: Swapping Schedule III (plants) with Schedule IV (CITES)

claimText: The National Board for Wildlife, established under the Wildlife Protection Act, is chaired by the Prime Minister.
evidenceExcerpt: Establishes the National Board for Wildlife, chaired by the Prime Minister.
examUse: Institution-feature question identifying the chairperson of statutory environmental bodies.
distractorHooks: Chaired by the Minister of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
Chaired by the Chief Wild Life Warden

claimText: The Chief Wild Life Warden is authorized to permit the hunting of Schedule I and II animals if they pose a threat to human life or are diseased beyond recovery.
evidenceExcerpt: Chief Wild Life Warden can permit hunting of Schedule I and II animals under Sections 11 and 12 if they are dangerous to human life or diseased beyond recovery.
examUse: Statement-based question on exceptions to the prohibition of hunting protected species.
distractorHooks: Only the Central Government can permit hunting of Schedule I animals
Schedule I animals can never be hunted under any circumstances

claimText: Prior permission from the Central Government is mandatory for the translocation of Schedule I animals.
evidenceExcerpt: Translocation of Schedule I animals requires prior permission from the Central Government.
examUse: Statement-based question on the regulatory approvals required for wildlife management.
distractorHooks: Translocation requires permission only from the State Government
Translocation requires permission from the National Board for Wildlife
